foundation.title: STRUCT
foundation.subtitle: Data Records
foundation.content: A C++ Struct can hold multiple variables of different types. In C++, a Struct is almost identical to a 'Class', just with public default access.
foundation.scenario: A Student ID Card. It holds a Photo (Binary), a Name (Text), and a Number (Integer). One card is one person.

examples.title: Creation
examples.explanation: Defining a blueprint for a new record type using 'struct'.
examples.code: struct Player { int hp; char id; };

examples.title: Accessing
examples.explanation: Using a dot ('.') to see exactly one thing from inside the record.

examples.title: Self Methods
examples.explanation: C++ allows شما to put functions directly inside the Struct's data.

foundation.keyPoint: struct allows multi-type containers. Default is Public.

foundation.title: ENUM
foundation.subtitle: Named Constants
foundation.content: An Enum allows you to create a list of named numbers (like states) instead of using raw integers like 0, 1, 2.
foundation.scenario: A Traffic Light. Instead of using 0 for Red and 1 for Green, you use the actual names RED, YELLOW, GREEN.

examples.title: Basic List
examples.explanation: Creating a map of human-readable words instead of numbers.

examples.title: Usage
examples.explanation: Setting a variable specifically to one of the named states.

examples.title: Switch Case
examples.explanation: Using a Switch to check each state's name precisely and cleanly.

foundation.title: HEADERS
foundation.subtitle: Code Blocks
foundation.content: C++ uses Header files (.h) to share logic between different .cpp files in a massive engine.
foundation.scenario: A Shared Recipe Book. Every chef (file) can include (include) the recipes (logic) in the .h file.

examples.title: Standard Lib
examples.explanation: Importing a box of pre-made tools from the official C++ library.

examples.title: Local One
examples.explanation: Including a file you wrote yourself in the same folder with quotes.

examples.title: The Guards
examples.explanation: Using #pragma once to prevent C++ from reading the same file twice.
